sentences.text: As I was saying-- is they were chasing me by bicycle.
And one of them kept on continuously beating me, the, the Hungarian one.
And I fell numerous times.
And I had to somehow get up with my hands tied in the back.
And after we got to the synagogue-- that's where they were collecting all the Jews, as I said before, that they-- somebody put some kind of complaint.
Apparently, the non-Jewish community already had all their complaints submitted to somebody.
Because everybody knew somebody who they wanted to bring up.
All you needed is not to be Jewish.
You could put any complaint against anybody you wanted as long as the person that they put the complaint in was Jewish.
Of course, I got to this-- to the temple.
And I hear this horrible screaming there already.
And there is an officer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] turned around from me.
And the, the German officer goes over there.
And he talks to this-- the German soldier, that is, goes over-- the SS trooper goes over to the officer, who was-- I didn't see because his back was to me-- and talks to him.
And then the German officer turned around.
And he happened to be my classmate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [CRYING] he looked at me.
And I was beaten up.
And the-- actually, the SS trooper asked permission that I should be taken to the cemetery and shot, dig my own-- he, he, he was-- he, he came with a shovel.
I should dig my own grave.
And he would shoot me.
But the German officer was a classmate, looked at me.
He said, leave this guy alone.
Let me-- let me do what I have to do with him.
And then he, he called me by my first name.
He says, let me put you inside the synagogue.
And they, they-- although there were other German soldiers or Hungarians beating people up, he put me in a place that, at that time, didn't touch me anymore.
And at the end of the day, he came over to me and talked to me for about 15 minutes.
And he warned me, tell your family to do anything they can.
Because we had a farm.
And, and he said that we should just promise the guard that he can have the farm after the war, that he should save our whole family.
And we should go there.
We should go up to the mountains.
I should organize.
Anyway, because the Russians are so close, that within a month they going to be here.
But they will take us away.
And they are taking them.
And I remember the first time I heard that name, [GERMAN].
That is a-- I don't even know how to translate that-- extermination camp, OK?
And a Hungarian gendarme took me and took me into the ghetto.
And the ghetto--
Schlier, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hans Schlier.
[CRYING] Yeah, that cracks me up.
I don't think he survived the war, either.
Who knows?
With me, he was very nice.
But he actually saved my life.
And not only that, he, he told me that I should try to save my family somehow.
And I spoke to my father.
Nobody believed it, OK?
In the meantime, I did go to the-- to the-- this man who was the manager of our farm.
And I told him, hey, you can have a-- not only that, I signed the paper.
And by the way, he used that paper.
But he said, I don't have to do anything.
He said, you, you, you-- you're going to leave the farm for us anyway.
Because they're going to take you away.
He seemed to know that.
And when I came back to my parents, they pursued it, but not seriously.
And they were always afraid.
I still had my father's parents and my father's-- one of the brothers.
And I had my mother's brother, who were also in the ghetto.
And they felt that, yeah, they will take us.
Because the Germans were saying that they will put us into work camps and repatriate till the war ends because we are not trusted [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people.
Anyway, I didn't see it that way.
Because to be not trusted is one thing.
And to be brutal and beating and shooting was another thing.
So I never believed them for a minute.
So when I was in the ghetto, I started organizing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because once I got in there with this gendarme who took me in there, I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somehow felt, how do I get organized to get out of here?
I told you-- is-- I already went to see this guy on the farm.
I snuck out of the ghetto at night.
And I saw him.
And he was not willing to do anything.
Then--
The ghetto?
The ghetto was a, a roped-off section of the city, where most of the Jewish people lived.
We didn't live in that part.
And then they, from the surrounding villages, brought in at least half as many or maybe more, more Jews from the little villages.
I think they brought in another 8,000 to 10,000 Jews.
And they jammed them in to an area that probably 1,000 people could live comfortably.
So it was terribly, terribly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] congested.
And we-- somehow, we-- this-- we had a couple of families, non-Jewish families, who helped us bring food.
So that's how we survived till the transport was taken to Auschwitz.
However, I tried to organize a group.
Where did you sleep?
On the floor.
I slept on the floor in this place where I-- it's interesting that when the gendarme took me from the temple into the ghetto, I went to the-- well, it was like a family friend.
But their daughter was my father's assistant, also.
And at the beginning, I was the only one moving into their house.
Because their house happened to be in within the perimeter of the ghetto.
And then, later on, my parents moved in there, also.
While I was there, I needed someone-- I, I was-- during the day, I was afraid to go out of the ghetto because I didn't want this guy to see me again and to know what happened to me.
So I send somebody who lived in that area for my father.
Because we had-- my father collected quite a-- an amount of gold.
Because don't forget, they make teeth out of gold at that time.
And that was the value that you could purchase things with it.
So I sent this boy to get some of it because I was going to purchase with that certain survival armor-- survival equipment, namely arms.
I would be able to already get that.
And it so happened-- is I wound up with a revolver and a automatic handgun, two guns, and also a .22-caliber rifle with ammunition.
But I got that free from somebody who-- in the city, a non-Jew who was very sympathetic to-- a very liberal individual, who was a partner with a Jewish lady.
And they owned a photography shop.
And he obtained the two handguns and one rifle for me.
And I smuggled it in at night to the ghetto.
Chisik.
I remember the name.
And the lady, the partner's name was Kastenbaum.
Anyway, I took it in.
And at that time, I remember I put the rifle with the two handguns in a barn, upstairs from a barn, in the hay.
I hid it.
And I tried to organize people to go up the mountain-- because we had a small mountain in a forest-- and to hide there.
Because I, again, told them-- is that-- what Schlier told me.
And that-- let's try to save as, as much as we can.
However, I organized about a group of four or five people.
I was the youngest one.
And then, the Hungarians came, and that everybody who was born up to 1923-- these were people one year older than me-- were entitled to go in-- eligible.
Or they were being drafted into the labor service.
So four of my five people that I organized were taken away.
So here I was left alone.
Then I went to this Kastenbaum lady.
And we try to organize to have a-- we already knew that.
And, and I told you Schlier told me that, sooner or later they going to take us away.
So I tried to organize.
And I tried to get my sister, also, into a, a bunker.
So we were looking for basements.
And this Kastenbaum found a, a place where to go.
And we tried to organize who would be going with us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she found the place.
It's somebody by the name [NON-ENGLISH], where there was a basement that was build with brick.
And it had a door that if we closed that in, similar to the brick outside, nobody would know that that's a room.
Because it was sort of jutted out-- jutted out of the building.
It was actually underground.
So the wall would remain flat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I tried to get-- she was going to go there.
And I thought my sister would go there.
The only sister that I-- survived, Sidi.
Her name is now Sidi Glock, Mrs., Mrs.
Glock.
At that time, it was Sidi Grunstein.
And for whatever reason, I honestly don't remember now why, she wound up going with my family.
I think my father felt more comfortable having her with him-- and then they keep the family together-- than go with me into the basement.
So then that Kastenbaum lady decided not to go.
And we-- I wound up with three other people.
And one of them lives here in California now.
His name is-- it used to be [?
Rosenberg. ?] But now it's Roland.
And he came in with us.
We collected-- I went outside.
We bought all kind of smoked ham, smoked foods, bread, some oil, and I don't remember-- vegetables.
But, you know, as you know, they don't stay too long.
And when they took-- the, the, the Jews were removed in trains to Auschwitz in three transports.
Because I believe there were about 12,000 or 14,000 inhabitants.
So each train, they had, like, I don't know, a couple hundred people in one wagon.
I'm, I'm not sure.
I-- because I did not see it.
I did not go out.
Anyway, I, I like to backtrack a little bit.
I tried to organize.
You know, don't forget, I had these weapons.
I tried to organize a group to go up the mountain.
And by that time, four of the five people were taken away.
And the fifth one was one of them also-- who eventually left for the labor service.
But I sent them to get some gold from my father.
My father gave him a box full of gold.
And he came back.
And he said that the gendarmes took it away from him.
Obviously, he lied.
But such is life.
So then that whole thing fell apart.
I was just afraid that if he does that, he might also give me up to the authorities that I had weapons.
And therefore, I was never be-- able to get back to the place that I hid them.
Because they took away the people from there.
And there was already roped off as a section.
They, they, they were taking all the people section by section.
And I was in the middle.
And they took the first transport from one end, and then the other transport from the other end.
And then they took the middle.
And the middle is where this bunker was made.
And we had it prepared.
And we took a Jewish mason, and we had him-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after they-- I saw nothing was happening.
I couldn't go to the mountain.
I, I took these three other people.
And we went into this basement, where the guy, the day before-- they were taken away on Fridays or Sundays.
I really don't remember now.
I think it was on a Friday.
And on Thursday afternoon, he walled us in and wished us good luck.
And we were there for approximately a little over three months.
1944, From some-- the end of May.
So that would have been June, July, and August.
We were there for about three months.
But we had food only for about six weeks.
And we tried to stretch it.
And the big problem was, although the food was a little getting spoiled, but we had no-- we ran out of water.
But we had a lot of wine and water in jars.
But we had enough only for six weeks.
So although we rationed-- at the end, when we saw nothing is happening yet, we started digging for water.
And we, we, we had small implements-- shovels without a handle.
And we-- I think we dug down about 8, 8 to 10 feet, and no water.
There was just no, no, no water well coming.
So we waited till the end of August.
And then we felt--
Well, anyway, to go back, sometime at the end of July, we heard shots.
And crowds were, were screaming.
And oh, I also forgot to tell you one thing-- that the building, when the Jews were taken away, the German headquarters of the-- of, of, of the area moved in on top of us.
So actually, I heard everything what was going on, the movements, where the Russians were coming, when they pulling back.
I heard everything downstairs.
Because the floor was made out of wood.
But there was no, no thickness to insulate the voice.
And that must have been the room-- right above us must have been the room where they did all the planning.
Nobody lived there.
So during the day, we were quiet.
And in the evening, they left.
And that's when we made all our movements, eating, sleeping, whatever you had to do.
Of course, we also dug a hole for a toilet.
And that's what we used all the way to the end.
So we lived with the food, the smell, and everything for about three months.
Four.
I'm sorry, three-- no, four.
One and three more-- so it's four.
One was, I told you, is-- who lives here by the name of [?
Rosenberg, ?] who is now Roland.
And the other two were a brother and sister.
And their name is Freid, Ilona-- one.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, and the brother's name, who was one year older than me, was-- or perhaps my age, was Jani Fried.
And four of us were there.
No.
We just had-- we, we, we dug through the foundation of the building.
We dug a little hole that wound up in shrubs.
The next-door neighbor was a, a doctor's office.
And he had a nice flower garden.
And among, among the rose bushes, we dug a hole.
And, and we-- that we used for air.
We had actually two little holes.
So we had a little ventilation.
But they were very small, just enough so the air will be able to get in there.
And we even could see out, sometimes, German soldiers coming and going.
And again, we spend there the three months.
And then somewhere in August-- that I'm going to backtrack again.
We heard these shots.
And we heard a room of people screaming, yelling.
And we didn't know what was going on.
But then at the end of August, when we ran out of food-- mainly water we ran out of.
We had no water.
We had to leave.
But sometimes, in the middle of August, we noticed that the-- moving, movement.
The Germans moved on.
So we figured that the Russians must be very near because we heard the artillery shells exploding already.
And we figured we'd get out, and somewhere we'd hide.
And, and we'll be-- we'll survive that way.
Anyway, it took us about three, four days that we removed enough huge rocks from the foundation, so we could somehow get through.
Because the-- this Ilona was a little stockier.
To get her through was a little more difficult than getting me through.
I was quite skinny at the time, OK?
So I was the first one to be out.
I went and looked around, if I-- we see anything.
I saw nothing.
And then I went for them.
And I sort of whispered to get them out.
Because during the day, we still heard soldiers going back and forth.
Although we knew they, they moved-- but we didn't know if they had any guards or didn't have any guards.
So when we got all the three people out, I was the first one to sort of like a scout, seeing if there are any guard.
And I wanted to go up to the mountain.
In our hometown, they had a lot of vineyards.
And I figured if I go up to the-- to the top of the mountain, we'll be able to feed ourselves with water and, and grapes.
Because that was the-- the end of August is already the grape season.
However, I got out, and I saw nobody.
But the other three made too much noise.
And a Hungarian guard came.
And all the men-- with the rifles shaking.
And I kept on saying, run.
I didn't want to leave.
I don't want to abandon the other three.
So we were caught.
And the guard already is telling them, don't worry-- that the Jews are safe now already.
We have a new government who left all the Jews in Budapest.
And therefore, you will have no problem.
And I listened.
And we-- they took us and gave us over to the gendarmes again.
And the gendarmes took us to jail.
And sure enough, the local people-- the same people who were so nasty and so beating people up-- kept on bringing a basket of food.
And one of them, who was my father's patient, he said to me, you know something, there is no dentist in town-- and that your father is OK.
They are receiving letters.
They are on a relocation center.
And they'll be all right.
I somehow didn't believe it.
The other people did believe it.
Anyway, they treated us quite well.
And then, for some reason or other, they said they're going to take us to Kassa.
That's Kosice.
And we probably stayed there because there's no Jews in the area.
And the Germans are still in charge over the Jews in Hungary.
However, the Hungarian government-- there was a buddy named Lakatos-- was quite tolerant of the Jews.
And they kept them in Budapest.
In other-- in other words, they, they stop the deportation from Budapest.
And they said, most likely, they're going to take us to Budapest.
But that's not what happened.
They were putting us on a train and shipping us to Kosice.
That meant that they'd taken us to Auschwitz.
So I was looking already.
But we were chained to one another.
Then, when we got to a city by the name Beregszasz, gendarmes came up and took us from-- off the train.
We didn't know why.
And then somehow, the, the local police-- there were the gendarmes and the city police.
The local police were a little more friendly.
And they were telling us that they just-- the Americans bombed the, the train station in Kosice, in Kassa, and that they totally devastated it.
And they were going to take us to, to Kassa to some concentration camp or relocation lager.
But we can't go there.
Therefore, we're going to stay here for a day or two.
They can repair it.
They'll take us there.
If not, they'll take us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Budapest.
And we were there about two, three days.
And they didn't feed us there.
No, they, they were not so friendly as the people in my hometown.
But I spoke to one of the local gendarmes.
And I told them that if you save us, then I will find some gold that was hidden by somebody.
I don't know where it is from here.
But I, I will find it.
And I'll reward you for that.
And he said that he cannot do it all four of us.
But he'll try to do one or two.
But anyway, this negotiation got nowhere, because then, once-- early in the morning, they took us, put us on a train.
And they shipped us to Budapest.
And in Budapest, I was in the [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know if you know that.
Did you ever hear of the [NON-ENGLISH]?
[NON-ENGLISH] was a center of war criminals, where they brought you.
And then they put you in jail.
If you were a, a non-Jew, they put you in jail.
If you were a Jew, they shipped you through to Auschwitz.
And we-- they took us there.
And that was an awful place.
It's terribly liced up.
Everything was filled-- all kind of criminals, although-- and, and, and Jews.
And one of these-- [?
Rosenberg's ?] sister was already in Budapest.
And she was friendly with German officers.
And one of the German officers came and took him and the two others out, but not me.
I don't know why.
I have no idea till today.
But the same day, I said, I have to solve this problem.
And I-- they asked people to volunteer for work.
And I volunteered for work because I figured, what are you going to work in here?
They must take you out.
And then if they take me out, I'll see how I can jump.
Anyway, that's not what happened.
What happened is I had to load-- we had to load on a truck, books.
We loaded them up.
And I remained on the truck, hidden in the truck, went out of there.
I jumped out and went to the Jewish Community Center.
We already, in the-- that [NON-ENGLISH], heard that they are active and that they are an organized group there.
And they are in the Dohany Templom.
That was the main temple in Budapest.
And all I had to do is get there.
This was end of August, beginning of September.
And that the Lakatos regime, if they-- you were caught outside the [NON-ENGLISH], at best they will wind up being in the Jewish ghetto.
Because they also had a ghetto in Budapest.
And they moved in all the Jewish people from the surrounding area.
In the, the main headquarters, where they had the Jewish-- I'm sure you heard this story before-- they always appointed the elders in the Jewish community to-- called them the Judenrat.
To-- and they had their own Judenrat.
But below the Judenrat, there was an organization that-- it was already organizing.
And they were funded by outside sources, with whom I was involved later on.
Anyway, I ran away.
And I-- wound up that I met my sister's Hebrew teacher, who was the main guy in organizing these groups, by the name Bilitzer.
And I told him my story.
And he took a liking to me right away.
And he said that you're going to work with us.
And he made fake papers for me as a lieutenant in the Hungarian Army, and-- with birth certificate I had, and everything with me.
It was all done.
And it happened to be somebody from my area.
So in case they asked me, I should know.
Not only that, I studied for about three days Latin prayers, in case they catch me.
You know, there are two things they would do, is first, they will test you, what religion you are?
And they are going to ask you some of the religious prayers.
And I-- it turned out to be that was a genuine birth certificate that I had.
The only thing-- it was not mine.
The picture was also genuine.
But the, the internal pass and the army pass was fake.
And, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got dressed as that.
And I went around looking where to help Jewish people.
Because they-- from the [NON-ENGLISH] they still were taking people to Buda.
And from Buda, they were walking into Vienna by foot.
And I was supposed to go with all kind of orders and take so many people away, mainly-- they were mainly women.
The men were not there.
Women and children-- they got names of people who were taken away.
And I was-- be able to-- they, they made false papers from the Ministry of Interior or the police.
And we went there in uniform.
I was the lieutenant.
And I had two other soldiers with me.
And we took them.
We were saying, these people stole typewriters-- I'll never forget the one of them.
And therefore, they are responsible to the police.
And I am part of-- and we took these people out.
And we took them into safe houses, those days, that we had.
